title: Expressing Arguments in Mwotlap: Word Order, Pronouns, and Object Markers
introduction: In linguistic typology, many languages mark participant roles (who does what to whom) through verbal agreement, morphological case, or voice alternations (e.g. active vs. passive). English, for example, uses word order (Subject-Verb-Object) and limited case marking on pronouns (he sees him vs. he sees him) but also passive constructions to shift roles (He was seen by him). By contrast, Mwotlap displays no verbal agreement or passive voice. Instead, it relies on: (1) fixed SVO order; (2) invariant verb roots; (3) free pronouns or noun phrases for each argument; and (4) small pre-verbal particles (te, me) to introduce objects or obliques. The combination of linear order, particles, and an elaborate pronominal system allows Mwotlap speakers to encode all participant roles without inflecting the verb.

example.description: An intransitive predicate melep “understand” with the speaker pronoun no marking the sole participant (agent/experiencer).

description.description: In Mwotlap, verbs do not inflect for person. Each participant—agent, patient, beneficiary—is expressed by a free pronoun or noun phrase. Roles are licensed by their linear order and by small particles; the verb root itself remains unchanged.

sections.focus: Fixed SVO order and absence of morphological case

example.description: A transitive clause: subject no “I” precedes the verb m-et “saw”, and the object kōyō “them” follows.

description.description: Mwotlap signals subjects and objects through a fixed subject-verb-object order. There is no case marking on nouns; their syntactic function is determined solely by position in the clause.

sections.focus: Light verbs and kernel event roots

example.target_sentence: tō kē nilep te kis me hiy no so nok so gen egal
example.source_sentence: He gave me the fruits to try.
example.description: Ditransitive give-verb nilep preceded by linker tō, with agent kē “he”, patient te kis “the fruits”, beneficiary me hiy no “to me”, and so…egal “to try” as a verbal complement.

description.description: Core verbs in Mwotlap are nominal or event roots often preceded by light verbs or linkers such as so, te, or tō. These linkers help integrate verbal complements or serial verbs.

example.source_sentence: Have you eaten something particular lately?
example.description: Transitive eat-verb megen with nēk “you” as agent, nahapqiyig “something” as patient, and qualifier tegha following the object.

description.description: Patient/theme arguments appear after the predicate, often introduced by small pre-verbs like te or me. These particles mark direct objects and other oblique roles without morphological case.

sections.focus: Pronominal system: person, number, and clusivity

example.description: Pronoun gēn (1 inclusive pl) as subject precedes the future marker tu-wuh.

description.description: Mwotlap pronouns distinguish person (1st/2nd/3rd), number (sg/du/tr/pl), and first-person clusivity (inclusive vs. exclusive). Their linear position around the predicate indicates their syntactic function.

example.target_sentence: tō kē nilep te kis me hiy no so nok so gen egal
example.source_sentence: He gave me the fruits to try.
example.description: Here, the beneficiary “to me” is expressed by me hiy no, a free pronoun phrase after the verb.

description.description: Secondary arguments such as beneficiaries are expressed as free pronouns or NPs placed after the core verb phrase, often without additional marking beyond position.

example.description: The incorporated form mē-tēqtēq ‘shoot.dup-parrot’ combines the object “parrot” within the verb.

description.description: In some cases, the object noun can be incorporated into the verb stem, resulting in a single morphological unit that encodes both action and patient.

example.source_sentence: The wind blew the cards away.
example.description: Two intransitive verbs lem “wind” and mi-yip “blow” cooperate to express a transitive action without conjunction.

description.description: Serial verb constructions allow multiple verbs to sequence actions or encode complex relationships. All verbs share the same argument structure and maintain SVO order.

example.source_sentence: My father is a fierce man.
example.description: Equative predicate n-et “is” with subject imam mino “my father” and predicate noun maymay “fierce man”.

description.description: Mwotlap employs the same SVO/default order for non-verbal predicates, including equative and ascriptive constructions, with a copular element introducing the predicate N.

example.source_sentence: A stone has cut my finger.
example.description: An actor noun phrase ne-vet vitwag “a stone” precedes the verb me-hel “cut”, and patient na-mnē-k “my finger” follows.

description.description: Mwotlap follows a nominative-accusative alignment: the subject of both transitive and intransitive verbs occupies the same position (pre-verbal). There is no passive voice; actors always precede the predicate.

example.description: The same pronoun kōyō occurs as both agent and patient around mu-wuh “hit”, illustrating reflexive/reciprocal ambiguity.

description.description: Mwotlap lacks dedicated reflexive or reciprocal pronouns. Ordinary pronouns are reused for both roles, and context disambiguates the intended meaning.

conclusion: Remember: Mwotlap verbs never inflect for person or number. Instead, fixed SVO order, free pronouns/NPs, and small pre-verbal particles (te, me) license all participant roles. Light verbs/linkers (so, te, tō), object incorporation, and serial verb constructions enrich the expression of complex events, while non-verbal predicates maintain the same syntactic pattern.

translation_drills.source: I understand.
Have you eaten something particular lately?

translation_drills.target: tō kē nilep te kis me hiy no so nok so gen egal
translation_drills.source: He gave me the fruits to try.
I saw them.
They saw me.
A stone has cut my finger.
We will kill Vēnvēntey tomorrow.
They killed them/themselves.
I hunted a parrot.
The wind blew the cards away.
My father is a fierce man.

translation_drills.target: Nēk so vasem lō qēt van so na-hap ni-akakteg geh.
translation_drills.source: You must tell me everything that's going on.
Eh! Something's happening to the old man!

translation_drills.target: Kimi me-lep nē-sēm a et-vēhge te no.
translation_drills.source: You took the money without asking me.
Who taught you that?

translation_drills.target: nē-qētēnge a kēy mu-wuh tō no aē en
translation_drills.source: The stick with which they hit me.

translation_drills.target: Nok soksok e intik gēn! Akē nēk me-lep tō kē?
translation_drills.source: I was just looking for my baby! Actually was it you who took it?

translation_drills.target: Wun n-age gōs kē a ni-wsewseg n-et en!
translation_drills.source: So it's this animal that was wreaking havoc among the men!
Mum had already forbidden that to you.

translation_drills.target: Mino, ak hiy no qele so na-halvan.
translation_drills.source: Darling, you treated me like a stranger.

translation_drills.target: No m-ak sisgoy nu-qul, tō imam mo-boel aē.
translation_drills.source: I dropped the lamp, which made my father angry.
Don't hold it against me!

translation_drills.target: Nēk so m-age, nitog vavasem na-halgoy en.
translation_drills.source: Whatever happens, don't give away our secret.

translation_drills.target: Nēk tog ak magaysēn wōlōm̄gep kē, van lok me! Tog ak magaysēn kē!
translation_drills.source: Stop harming this young man, come back here! Stop harassing him!

translation_drills.target: Nēk a m-ekas me kē en.
translation_drills.source: You're the one who found her.

translation_drills.target: Bulsal mino, tog ak magaysēn no!
translation_drills.source: Hey, my friend, don't hurt me!

translation_drills.target: No-wotva, am̄ag, kēy ha na-naw aē.
translation_drills.source: A long time ago, calabashes were used to draw water from the sea.
They did me wrong nonetheless.
I can feel something biting me.
